name: Mathematical Induction — The Domino Proof
whatIsIt: The PRINCIPLE OF MATHEMATICAL INDUCTION (PMI) proves a statement P(n) is true for EVERY natural number n, using just two steps. BASE CASE: show P(1) is true. INDUCTIVE STEP: ASSUME P(k) is true (the assumption), then PROVE P(k+1) is true. If both hold, P(n) is true for all n — like a line of dominoes where the first falls and each one knocks over the next.
whyNeeded: Many formulas — sums of series, divisibility results, inequalities — hold for all natural numbers, and you cannot check infinitely many. PMI proves them all at once. It is a Class 11 NCERT topic and a JEE Main favourite.
realLifeUses: A line of dominoes: knock the first, each topples the next
Proving a number is divisible by something for all n
Climbing a ladder: reach rung 1, and each rung lets you reach the next

vocabulary.meaning: Proving the statement true for the first value, usually n = 1.
The assumption that P(k) is true for some k.
Using the assumption P(k) to prove P(k+1).

story.title: Meena's Dominoes
story.text: Meena lines up dominoes and asks, 'How do I know they'll ALL fall?' Sir smiles. 'Two things. One: the first one falls (base case). Two: each falling domino knocks the next (inductive step). If both are true, every domino falls — even a thousand.' Meena flicks the first — and the whole line tumbles. 'That,' says Sir, 'is mathematical induction.'
story.extractedProblem: To prove a statement true for all natural numbers by induction, which two things must Meena show?
story.answerInStory: The base case (P(1) true) and the inductive step (P(k) true ⇒ P(k+1) true).

lines.label: Rung 1 true → each rung pushes the next → all rungs

visual.caption: Induction is a ladder: prove rung 1 (base case), and prove each rung leads to the next (inductive step). Then every rung is reachable.

teachingGallery.title: The two steps
teachingGallery.note: Base case, then the inductive step.

lines.label: Step 1: prove P(1) — the base case

examples.caption: First domino: show P(1) is true.
Each domino knocks the next: P(k) ⇒ P(k+1).

standardMethod.summary: Prove the base case P(1). Then assume P(k) is true and use it to prove P(k+1). Conclude P(n) holds for all natural n.
steps: BASE CASE: substitute n = 1 and verify the statement P(1) is true.
INDUCTIVE HYPOTHESIS: assume the statement P(k) is true for an arbitrary natural number k.
INDUCTIVE STEP: using that assumption, prove P(k+1) is true.
Conclude by PMI that P(n) is true for every natural number n.

workedExamples.problem: Use the formula 1+2+…+n = n(n+1)/2 to find the sum for n = 5.
The sum of the first n odd numbers equals n². Find it for n = 4.
steps: Sum of first n odd numbers = n².

commonMistakes.mistake: Proving only the inductive step and skipping the base case.
commonMistakes.fix: PMI needs BOTH: prove the base case P(1) AND the inductive step. Without the first domino, nothing falls.

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming P(k+1) (what you must prove) instead of assuming P(k).
commonMistakes.fix: Assume P(k) is true, then PROVE P(k+1). Never assume the thing you are trying to prove.

easy.q: Mathematical induction has how many steps?
hintLadder: First domino + each knocks the next.

easy.q: The FIRST step of induction is the…

medium.q: In the inductive step we assume P(k) and prove…

challenge.q: Sum of the first 4 odd numbers (= 4²)?
PMI proves a statement true for…

questions.q: The two steps of PMI are the base case and the…
In the inductive step you ASSUME that…
Sum of the first 6 odd numbers (= 6²)?

teachBackPrompt: Set up a row of coins or books like dominoes. Explain to a grown-up the two things induction needs — the first one falls (base case) and each one knocks the next (inductive step) — and why together they prove ALL of them fall.

revisionCard.summary: PMI proves P(n) for all natural n in two steps: (1) BASE CASE — prove P(1); (2) INDUCTIVE STEP — assume P(k), prove P(k+1). Both together ⇒ true for all n. Like dominoes: first falls, each knocks the next.

meta.curriculum: CBSE Class 11 / JEE Main (Principle of Mathematical Induction)

rememberIt.hook: Dominoes: prove P(1), then P(k) ⇒ P(k+1)
rememberIt.unpack: Base case starts it; inductive step carries it to every n. Never skip the base case.

studentQuestions.q: Why isn't the inductive step alone enough?
studentQuestions.a: It only says 'if one falls, the next falls' — but if NO domino ever falls (no base case), the chain never starts.

studentQuestions.q: Do we always start at n = 1?
studentQuestions.a: Usually, but the base case can be any starting value (like n = 0 or n = 2); then the result holds for all n from there.

formulas.name: Sum of first n naturals
formulas.remember: half of n times the next number
formulas.whenToUse: A classic statement to prove by induction.

formulas.name: Sum of first n odd numbers
